text: Welcome to another installment of robotic hernia surgery. Today, we're discussing the management of a traumatic suprapubic hernia. This was a woman that was referred to me for an inguinal hernia causing urinary symptoms. But on closer review, it was quite obvious that this was actually a ventral hernia related to her prior

emergency orthopedic interventions, which had been done to fixate her broken pelvis. On CT review, what you'll note is a significant amount of orthopedic hardware on the pubis with herniation of the bladder anterior to the pubis and in close proximity to all of that orthopedic hardware. She was evaluated by urology and ortho in advance of seeing me, and I plan to move forward with a robotic repair with them, sort of available should I need them.

The surgeon begins the initial dissection by creating a single flap from left to right, using techniques similar to those employed in bilateral inguinal hernia repair. A three-way bladder catheter is visible, aiding in the surgical setup.
The surgeon performs central retromuscular dissection, focusing on the area where the tissue flaps are thin and challenging to differentiate.

The surgeon performs lateral dissection before focusing centrally to reduce the hernia sac, which includes the bladder. On the patient's left side, careful dissection is conducted to identify an areolar plane, minimizing the risk of bladder injury.

As I mentioned earlier, a three-way bladder catheter was placed. I did inject ICG during the case, but unfortunately the catheter The imaging was not that great and not very helpful. Other things I consider in these scenarios should I be concerned about bladder injury intraop is distending the bladder with a solution of methylene blue to look for any dye.
The surgeon performs lateral dissection on the patient's right side, progressing towards the iliopsoas muscle. The dissection continues aggressively to expose the obturator canal on the right side.

The surgeon works on the left side, addressing the preperitoneal component of the round ligament. The dissection involves separating the bladder, which is in continuity with the pseudosac, from the anterior abdominal wall near the pubis. The procedure reveals orthopedic hardware as the bladder is carefully detached from the abdominal wall.

Our orthopedic colleagues were operating just down the hall, so I had them come in just to sort of get a view of this and double confirm that the loose hardware that we were seeing there was safe to remove and they agreed that it would be. So here we are, removal of the loose hardware something that you don't really get to do every day, but quite interesting and kind of fun to do with our ortho team in the room at the same time.
The surgeon completes a bilateral myopectin orifice dissection, achieving good exposure of the hernia. The bladder is reduced and distended to evaluate for leaks, with no obvious leaks observed.

The surgeon performs midline placation, addressing an avulsion injury where the rectus muscle is detached from the pubis. The procedure involves suturing along the midline, with plans to proceed to transverse placation similar to techniques used for direct hernia repair.

The surgeon performs transverse placation, similar to techniques used for direct hernias, to narrow the space by re-approximating the rectus muscle back to the pubis using multiple suture lines. Visible fascia on the pubis allows for secure attachment of the rectus muscle.
The video shows the reconstructed lower abdominal wall, resembling the view typically seen during bilateral inguinal hernia repairs.

The surgeon places overlapping mid-weight meshes over the groins using Bard 3D Max, and covers the central portion with a flat piece of polypropylene mesh measuring 20 by 15 centimeters. Mesh fixation is achieved with TIS seal and permanent sutures in the lower abdomen. The flap closure is initiated using three six-inch V-lock sutures, starting lateral to medial on both sides, with an independent suture line for the central portion, completing the flap closure.
The surgeon completes the exclusion of the mesh from the viscera and decompresses the preperitoneal space, marking the end of the procedure.
